testingFrequency: At installation, move, repair or replacement; annually for health-hazard assemblies
dueBasis: Arlington's backflow ordinance requires testing immediately after installation, when an assembly is moved, after repair or replacement, before re-occupancy after a year of vacancy, and at least annually for assemblies protecting against health hazards. The Regulatory Authority can require more frequent testing.
coveredPropertyTypes: Residential and commercial premises under Arlington's backflow ordinance
Lawn irrigation systems installed under City permit
Fire sprinkler and fire suppression systems
Properties with health-hazard or high-hazard assemblies
coveredDeviceTypes: Fire line DCVA, detector check, and RPZ assemblies
penalties: Arlington's backflow ordinance allows misdemeanor enforcement, treats each day a violation continues as a separate offense, and the ordinance history states violations can carry fines up to $2,000. The irrigation chapter separately carries enforcement and penalty provisions.
sourceExcerpt: Arlington publishes detailed backflow and irrigation ordinances rather than a consumer-oriented approved tester list. The code is explicit about testing triggers, City registration, irrigation permits, and fire line contractor rules.
verdictSummary: Arlington is a code-driven city: annual testing is tied to health-hazard assemblies, but installation, moves, repairs, replacement, irrigation permits, and fire line work all have separate ordinance triggers.
whoIsAffected: Residential and commercial properties with health-hazard assemblies, lawn irrigation systems, fire sprinkler systems, and premises that have been vacant long enough to trigger re-occupancy testing.
residentialNotes: Residential owners most often run into Arlington's rules through lawn irrigation permits and re-occupancy or repair testing triggers.
A homeowner should not flatten Arlington into a generic annual rule because the ordinance splits health-hazard annual testing from other event-based triggers.
commercialNotes: Commercial and other higher-hazard premises are much more likely to sit in the annual testing lane.
Fire protection, inspection access, and City registration requirements make Arlington more ordinance-heavy than a generic plumber quote suggests.

annualTesting.summary: Arlington's annual testing rule is specific: assemblies that protect against health hazards must be tested at least once a year, while other triggers come from installation, repair, replacement, moves, and long vacancy.
highlights: Health-hazard assemblies are tested at least annually.
Assemblies are also tested on installation, move, repair or replacement, and before re-occupancy after a year of vacancy.
Only certified testers registered with the City's Regulatory Authority can perform the work.
workflowSteps: Confirm whether the property sits in the health-hazard annual lane or an event-driven lane.
Put the results on the City form and keep the premises records current.

irrigation.summary: Arlington's irrigation workflow is permit-led: lawn irrigation systems need a building inspection permit and must comply with the plumbing code, the irrigation chapter, and the backflow chapter together.
highlights: All lawn irrigation systems require a permit from the Building Inspection Department.
Potable interconnections with alternate water sources are prohibited.
If reclaimed water is involved, Arlington requires both a reclaimed-water-side double check assembly and an RPZ on the potable side.
workflowSteps: Pull the irrigation permit before installation work begins.
Install the assembly to Arlington's plumbing, irrigation, and backflow code requirements.
Use the City testing and reporting path once the device is installed or later repaired.

fireLine.summary: Arlington is unusually explicit on fire line rules. Fire sprinkler systems use a City-approved DCVA as the minimum in many cases, but an RPZ is required when anything other than potable water can enter the system.
highlights: Fire systems with non-potable-approved piping or without periodic flow-through need at least a City-approved DCVA.
An RPZ is required when antifreeze, foam, or other non-potable solutions can enter the sprinkler system unless an air gap protects the tank.
Testing and repair on fire lines must be performed by personnel permanently employed by an approved fire line contractor.
workflowSteps: Confirm whether the fire system can stay on a DCVA or must move to an RPZ.
Install the fire line equipment through a state-licensed fire sprinkler system contractor.
Have a licensed fire line tester complete and submit the cross-connection test report to the Regulatory Authority.
Confirm whether the assembly is in Arlington's health-hazard annual lane or was triggered by installation, vacancy, repair, move, or replacement.
Use a certified tester registered with Arlington's Regulatory Authority.
If the work involves irrigation or fire protection, follow the separate permit and contractor rules before treating the job as routine testing.
Use the City's reporting form and keep assembly records on site.
failureHighlights: Arlington can require testing more frequently than the minimum annual rule when the Regulatory Authority deems it necessary.
Using an unregistered tester or ignoring a repair order creates ordinance exposure, not just scheduling delay.
Fire line repair work cannot be detached from approved fire line contractor requirements.

costBand.testingRange: $85 to $160 for a routine annual test
costBand.repairRetestRange: $250 to $1,350 depending on access and device type
costBand.pricingNotes: Arlington's public materials are stronger on ordinance triggers and contractor requirements than on retail pricing, so use local quotes rather than assuming the code reveals price.
